ip_addresses.evidence_snippet: Sample 12 first connects to a hard-coded IP address 149[.]248[.]11[.]71 on port 443 to blend in with normal web traffic. CISA observed the threat actors re-using this IP address.

urls.evidence_snippet: 'The sample uses DoH to resolve the address of its C2 servers by sending an encrypted HTTPS request to one of the following legitimate public DoH resolvers [T1071.001]: https[:]//1.0.0[.]1/dns-query (Cloudflare) https[:]//1.1.1[.]1/dns-query (Cloudflare) https[:]//8.8.4[.]4/dns-query (Google) https[:]//8.8.8[.]8/dns-query (Google) https[:]//9.9.9[.]9/dns-query (Quad9) If the C2 domain is not found in the public DoH resolver cache'

http_paths.evidence_snippet: Sample 12 first sends an HTTP upgrade request to convert the initial encrypted HTTPS connection into a persistent WebSocket connection via the endpoint /rest/apisession.

sigma_rules.name: BRICKSTORM Backdoor Activity r2
sigma_rules.evidence_snippet: title: BRICKSTORM Backdoor Activity r2. incident: 251157.r2. description: BRICKSTORM malware is a backdoor with multiple capabilities that threat actors use to set up persistence on exploited systems. Table 13.

ttps.evidence_snippet: The cyber actors accessed a web server inside a victim organisation's DMZ using a web shell [T1505.003] present on the server.
The modified init file controls the bootup process [T1037] on VMware vSphere systems and executes BRICKSTORM.

ttps.technique: Hijack Execution Flow: Path Interception by PATH Environment Variable
ttps.evidence_snippet: The parent BRICKSTORM instance modifies the PATH environment variable by appending the copied location's path [T1574.007]. This ensures the newly copied version of BRICKSTORM will be executed first.

ttps.technique: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Sudo and Sudo Caching
ttps.evidence_snippet: the cyber actors elevated privileges using the sudo command [T1548.003]
Some BRICKSTORM samples mimic legitimate names. For example, Sample 1, which was obtained from a VMware vSphere platform, is named vmsrc or vmware-sphere. [T1036]
The cyber actors used service account credentials [T1078] to move laterally using Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) [T1021.001] from the web server to a domain controller.
the cyber actors used service account credentials [T1078] to move laterally using Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) [T1021.001] from the web server to a domain controller in the DMZ.

the cyber actors used service account credentials to move laterally using RDP from the web server to a domain controller in the DMZ, from which they copied the Active Directory (AD) database (ntds.dit) [T1003.003].
BRICKSTORM can list directory contents on the compromised system. [T1083]
BRICKSTORM uses DoH to resolve the address of its C2 servers by sending an encrypted HTTPS request to one of the following legitimate public DoH resolvers [T1071.001].
The SOCKS Handler sets up a SOCKS proxy [T1090.001] to route C2 traffic and facilitate lateral movement within the victim network.
the cyber actors dropped BRICKSTORM malware in the server's /etc/sysconfig/ directory [T1105]
BRICKSTORM can upload files from the victim system to the cyber actors' C2 server. get-file: Downloads a file from the compromised system to the C2 server [T1041].

summary: Joint CISA/NSA/Canadian Centre for Cyber Security Malware Analysis Report (AR25-338A, initially published 4 December 2025, last updated 11 February 2026) on BRICKSTORM, a custom ELF backdoor (Go-, Rust-, and .NET AOT-based variants) attributed with high confidence to PRC state-sponsored cyber actors. Targets government services, facilities, and IT sectors. 12 samples analysed, primarily targeting VMware vSphere (vCenter, ESXi, Aria Automation Orchestrator) and Windows environments. At a victim organisation, actors gained persistent access from April 2024 through at least September 2025, using a web shell for initial access, RDP and SMB for lateral movement, sudo privilege escalation, ntds.dit credential dumping, and ADFS cryptographic key exfiltration. BRICKSTORM provides persistence via init script modification, PATH hijacking, and self-watching functions; C2 via DoH over public resolvers, HTTPS/WebSockets with nested TLS, and SOCKS proxy. One confirmed reused C2 IP: 149.248.11.71 (Sample 12). Seven YARA rules and one Sigma rule are provided for detection.

timeline.event: PRC cyber actors access victim organisation's DMZ web server via web shell
timeline.evidence_snippet: PRC state-sponsored cyber actors accessed a web server on April 11, 2024. The web server was inside the organization's demilitarized zone (DMZ), and cyber actors accessed it through a web shell [T1505.003] present on the server.

timeline.event: Lateral movement to internal domain controller; ntds.dit copied; BRICKSTORM deployed to VMware vCenter
timeline.evidence_snippet: On April 12, 2024, the cyber actors moved laterally from the web server to a domain controller within the internal network using RDP and credentials associated with a second service account.

timeline.event: Last observed BRICKSTORM persistent access at victim organisation
timeline.evidence_snippet: The cyber actors used BRICKSTORM for persistent access from at least April 2024 through at least Sep. 3, 2025.

timeline.event: Initial publication of BRICKSTORM Malware Analysis Report (AR25-338A)
MAR updated with analysis of Samples 9–11 (including two Rust-based samples)
timeline.evidence_snippet: Dec. 19, 2025, with IOCs and detection signatures for three additional BRICKSTORM samples. See Appendix C: Dec. 19, 2025, Updates and Table 12.

timeline.event: MAR updated with additional YARA detection signatures
timeline.evidence_snippet: Jan. 20, 2026, with detection signatures. See Table 12.

timeline.event: MAR updated with analysis of Sample 12 (.NET AOT variant) and reused C2 IP 149.248.11.71
timeline.evidence_snippet: Feb. 11, 2026, with analysis, IOCs, and detection signatures for an additional sample of BRICKSTORM. This sample is a different variant than the other samples. See Appendix D: Feb. 11, 2026, Updates.
